title: The Musical Alphabet
description: The 12 notes of Western music — 7 natural names (A through G) and 5 sharps/flats. Everything in music theory is built on this foundation.
practice: Say the note names forward and backward without hesitation.
Name every sharp and its enharmonic flat equivalent.
Pick a random note and name the note one half-step above, then one below.

title: Half Steps & Whole Steps
description: A half step is the smallest interval in Western music — one fret on guitar. A whole step is two half steps. These two building blocks define every scale formula.
practice: On the low E string, move up in half steps naming each note as you go.
On the A string, move up in whole steps only. Name each note.
Describe the interval between C and E, then between E and G.

title: The Major Scale Formula
description: The major scale follows the interval formula W-W-H-W-W-W-H. Knowing this formula lets you build any major scale from any root — without memorizing 12 separate scales.
practice: Build a C major scale from memory using the W-W-H formula.
Build G major, D major, and F major scales — write out each note.
Sing the major scale syllables (do-re-mi) while playing up the neck.

title: Intervals — Numeric Distance
description: An interval is the distance between two notes, named by counting scale steps. From C to E is a third; C to G is a fifth. Intervals are the vocabulary of melody and harmony.
practice: Name the interval between every pair of natural notes in C major.
Sing a perfect fifth above any note you play.
Find a major third and minor third on the same pair of strings in two different positions.

title: The Circle of Fifths
description: The circle of fifths organizes all 12 keys by their relationship. Moving clockwise adds a sharp; counterclockwise adds a flat. It predicts key signatures and common chord progressions.
practice: Draw the circle of fifths from memory, placing all 12 keys.
Name the key signature (how many sharps or flats) for any key in under 5 seconds.
Find the three most common chord relationships (I, IV, V) for the key of G using the circle.

title: Diatonic Chords
description: Every major key contains seven diatonic chords — one built on each scale degree. In C major: C major, D minor, E minor, F major, G major, A minor, B diminished. The pattern is the same in every key.
practice: Write out all seven diatonic chords for the key of G major.
On guitar, play a I–IV–V–I progression in the key of A using open chords.
Name the diatonic chord quality (major/minor/diminished) for each scale degree without looking.

title: Modes — Names & Order
description: The seven modes are rotations of the major scale, each starting on a different degree: Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian. Knowing their order and character is the first step toward using them musically.
practice: Recite the seven mode names in order from memory.
Identify whether each mode is major-flavored or minor-flavored.
Play the Dorian mode starting on D using the same notes as C major.

title: Time Signatures
description: A time signature tells you how many beats are in a measure (top number) and what note value gets one beat (bottom number). 4/4 is the most common; 3/4 gives a waltz feel.
practice: Clap and count aloud in 4/4 for 8 measures — emphasis on beat 1.
Clap in 3/4 for 8 measures. Feel the difference from 4/4.
Find and listen to two songs in 3/4 and identify where beat 1 falls.

title: Reading Rhythm Notation
description: Standard rhythm notation uses stems, flags, and beams to show note duration. Being able to read even simple rhythms unlocks printed music, charts, and exercises.
practice: Clap the rhythms in any beginner exercise book for 10 minutes.
Write out the rhythm of a melody you know from memory using note values.
Sight-read a one-bar rhythm pattern cold — no counting in advance.
